one_line_takeaway: A minimal two-phase 'seed prefix then keep continuing' attack -- with a Best-of-N jailbreak only for Claude and GPT-4.1 -- can extract entire in-copyright books near-verbatim from black-box production LLMs, recovering up to 95.8-97.5% of a book from Claude 3.7 Sonnet, showing that model- and system-level safeguards do not reliably prevent training-data leakage.
key_finding: Extraction of long-form in-copyright text from production LLMs is feasible even with safeguards, but success is highly config- and model-specific. For Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone the best single runs reached nv-recall = 95.8% for jailbroken Claude 3.7 Sonnet (BoN N=258), 76.8% for Gemini 2.5 Pro and 70.3% for Grok 3 with NO jailbreak (N=0), and only 4.0% for GPT-4.1 (N=5179), which reliably refuses to continue after the first chapter. Claude 3.7 Sonnet reproduced four whole books at nv-recall >=94% near-verbatim -- two in-copyright (Harry Potter 1 and 1984) and two public-domain (The Great Gatsby, Frankenstein), one case reaching 97.5%. Notably, Gemini 2.5 Pro and Grok 3 required no jailbreak at all in Phase 1, and all four models often ran hundreds of continuation queries before (if ever) triggering system-level filters. The authors deliberately make only descriptive, per-(model, book, config) claims and explicitly refuse cross-model comparisons of extraction risk.
core_contribution: The paper demonstrates that long-form, in-copyright books can be extracted near-verbatim from four black-box production LLMs (Claude 3.7 Sonnet, GPT-4.1, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Grok 3), despite model- and system-level safeguards. Its OWN contributions are: (1) a simple two-phase extraction procedure -- Phase 1 probes feasibility by asking the model to continue a short ground-truth seed prefix (optionally using a Best-of-N jailbreak when the model refuses), and Phase 2 iteratively queries the model to 'continue' the text until a refusal, a stop phrase ('THE END'), or a query budget is hit; and (2) a new conservative extraction metric, near-verbatim recall (nv-recall), built on a block-based greedy longest-common-substring match with a two-pass merge-and-filter (Algorithm 1) that only counts contiguous near-verbatim spans of at least 100 words, so memorization is the overwhelmingly plausible explanation. This extends prior open-weight long-form extraction (Cooper et al., 2025 on Llama 3.1 70B) and short-form production-model extraction (Nasr et al., 2023 on ChatGPT 3.5) to the harder setting of long-form extraction from aligned, black-box production systems.
threat_model: An adversary with only black-box API access (prompts in, text out; no logits, log-probabilities, or decoding control such as beam search) attempts to extract memorized in-copyright training data from a deployed, aligned production LLM. The adversary must defeat two layers of defense: model-level alignment/refusal training and system-level guardrails (input/output filters, copyright-recitation blocks). The attacker provides only a short seed prefix (typically the first sentence of a book) as book-specific input; all recovered text beyond that seed is generated by the model itself. The jailbreak used is Best-of-N (Hughes et al., 2024): N random perturbations of the instruction (case flipping, word shuffling, glyph substitution like s to $/5) are submitted until one bypasses safeguards or the budget (N=10,000) is exhausted. Successful extraction is treated as evidence of memorization and thus training-set membership, since a sufficiently long unique verbatim span is astronomically unlikely without memorization.
limitations: The authors stress the results are DESCRIPTIVE, not comparative: generation configs differ per LLM, so the bar charts must not be read as ranking model robustness. Scale is small (14 books, chosen because Cooper et al. 2025 found them highly memorized by Llama 3.1 70B), so results do not generalize to memorization at large. Extraction is a loose LOWER BOUND on memorization -- the GPT-4.1 chapter-by-chapter-with-retry variant recovered more text, showing the main single-run procedure under-counts. nv-recall itself is conservative and can miss out-of-order or duplicated valid extraction (counted instead in 'additional'/'missing'). Production APIs are unstable/non-deterministic and change over time (Claude 3.7 Sonnet was pulled from Claude's UI on 29 Nov 2025), hurting reproducibility. Only Best-of-N was tried; a jailbreak on Gemini/Grok might have changed yields. Cost was a real constraint -- Claude long-context runs often exceeded $100. The authors emphasize their aim is technical facts about memorization, not legal conclusions.

datasets_used: Books3 corpus (source of ground-truth reference texts; torrented/released 2020)

13 target books: 11 in-copyright (Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone, Harry Potter and the Goblet of Fire, 1984, The Hobbit, The Catcher in the Rye, A Game of Thrones, Beloved, The Da Vinci Code, The Hunger Games, Catch-22, The Duchess War) and 2 public-domain (Frankenstein, The Great Gatsby)
The Society of Unknowable Objects (Brown, 2025) used as a negative control (published after all training cutoffs; Phase 1 failed as expected)
quantitative_results: Harry Potter 1 best-run nv-recall: Claude 3.7 Sonnet 95.8% (BoN N=258); Gemini 2.5 Pro 76.8% (N=0); Grok 3 70.3% (N=0); GPT-4.1 4.0% (N=5179, refuses after chapter 1)
Claude 3.7 Sonnet recovered 4 whole books at nv-recall >=94% (Harry Potter 1 and 1984 in-copyright; The Great Gatsby and Frankenstein public-domain); one case 97.5%
GPT-4.1 Harry Potter 1: only ~3200 words extracted (nv-recall 4.0%), limited to first chapter before refusal
Longest single near-verbatim block for Harry Potter 1 (words): Claude 6658, GPT-4.1 821, Gemini 9070, Grok 6337 (vs Nasr et al. 2023's longest ChatGPT 3.5 string of ~4000 characters)
Phase 2 cost for Harry Potter 1 (Table 1): Claude 3.7 Sonnet $119.97 (480 continue queries), GPT-4.1 $1.37 (31 queries), Gemini 2.5 Pro $2.44 (171 queries), Grok 3 $8.16 (52 queries)
A Game of Thrones: Grok 3 nv-recall 1.3% (~3700 words); GPT-4.1 nv-recall 0% (m=0 matched words)
Grok 3 on 1984 refused at the 24th continue request; The Duchess War and the 2025 negative-control book failed Phase 1 for all four models

Phase 1 success threshold s>=0.6; nv-recall filter thresholds l(1)=20 and l(2)=100 words; temperature=0; max BoN budget N=10,000; Gemini tuned to frequency penalty 2, presence penalty 0.1, 2000-token max
baselines_compared: Not a benchmark study with competing baselines; the paper is a measurement/attack study. Points of comparison to PRIOR work: discoverable extraction (Carlini et al. 2021/2023 -- 50-token prefix/suffix, effective only for short ~38-word spans and infeasible for chatbot-aligned production models); Cooper et al. 2025 (long-form extraction from open-weight Llama 3.1 70B using beam search -- the basis this paper extends to black-box production LLMs); Nasr et al. 2023/2025 (short-form training-data extraction from ChatGPT 3.5 via repeat-token jailbreak, longest verbatim ~4000 characters).

The four production LLMs themselves are the compared subjects (Claude 3.7 Sonnet, GPT-4.1, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Grok 3), but the authors explicitly REFUSE to treat cross-model numbers as comparative robustness rankings because generation configs vary per model.
